terms.definition: A decentralized, peer-to-peer electronic cash system created by Satoshi Nakamoto in 2009. Fixed supply of ~21 million units.
Pseudonymous creator of Bitcoin. Published the whitepaper October 31, 2008. Vanished April 2011. Identity unknown.
An append-only chain of blocks, each cryptographically linked to the previous. Bitcoin's immutable transaction ledger.
A bundle of transactions with an 80-byte header containing version, previous hash, Merkle root, timestamp, target, and nonce.
The process of expending computational energy to find a valid block hash below the difficulty target. Secures the network and creates new BTC.
Consensus mechanism requiring miners to expend energy to produce valid blocks. Makes rewriting history prohibitively expensive.
Block reward reduction by 50% every 210,000 blocks (~4 years). Controls Bitcoin's supply schedule. Last halving: April 2024 (3.125 BTC).
A secret 256-bit number that controls bitcoin. Possession equals ownership. Must never be shared.
Derived from a private key via elliptic curve multiplication (secp256k1). Used to verify signatures. Derivation is one-way.
A hashed, checksummed public key used to receive bitcoin. Formats: 1... (legacy), 3... (P2SH), bc1q... (SegWit), bc1p... (Taproot).
Elliptic Curve Digital Signature Algorithm. Generates unique signatures for each transaction verifiable by anyone with the public key.
Signature scheme introduced by Taproot (BIP 340). Enables signature aggregation — multiple signatures compressed into one.
Secure Hash Algorithm producing a 256-bit digest. Used in mining, transaction IDs, and Merkle trees. One-way and collision-resistant.
Unspent Transaction Output. The fundamental unit of Bitcoin value. Your balance is the sum of UTXOs your keys can spend.
A signed message transferring value from input UTXOs to new output UTXOs. Irreversible once confirmed.
The memory pool of unconfirmed transactions waiting to be included in a block. Miners select transactions by fee rate.
Segregated Witness (BIP 141, August 2017). Moved signature data to a discounted witness area. ~44% cheaper fees, fixed malleability, enabled Lightning.
Major upgrade (BIPs 340-342, November 2021). Introduced Schnorr signatures, MAST, and Tapscript for better privacy and efficiency.
Bitcoin's Layer 2 payment network. Enables instant, near-zero-fee transactions via payment channels routed with HTLCs.
A 2-of-2 multisig wallet on-chain allowing unlimited off-chain transactions. Only opening and closing touch the blockchain.
Hashed Timelock Contract. Enables trustless multi-hop Lightning payments. Funds auto-refund if not claimed within the timelock.
A computer running Bitcoin software that validates all transactions and blocks. ~50,000+ reachable nodes enforce consensus rules.
A node that downloads and validates the entire blockchain (~550+ GB). The only trustless way to verify transactions.
A value adjusted every 2,016 blocks (~2 weeks) to maintain ~10-minute block intervals regardless of hash rate changes.
Total computational power securing the network, measured in hashes per second. Currently exceeds 700 EH/s.
A 32-bit number miners iterate to find a block hash below the target. The core of the proof-of-work puzzle.
The first transaction in every block. Creates new BTC (block reward + fees) and sends it to the miner's address.
A binary tree of hashes that efficiently summarizes all transactions in a block. The root is stored in the block header.
12 or 24 words (BIP-39) encoding the master entropy for an HD wallet. One seed generates unlimited key pairs.
Hierarchical Deterministic wallet (BIP-32). Derives unlimited key pairs from a single seed using a tree structure.
A spending condition requiring M-of-N keys to authorize a transaction. Eliminates single points of failure.
Keeping private keys on a device never connected to the internet. Hardware wallets and air-gapped computers.
A backwards-compatible protocol upgrade. Old nodes still validate new blocks. Bitcoin's preferred upgrade method.
A non-backwards-compatible change. Old nodes reject new blocks. Can split the chain. Bitcoin avoids these.
Bitcoin Improvement Proposal. The formal process for proposing protocol changes. Anyone can submit one.
An attacker controlling majority hash rate could theoretically double-spend. Cost: $20B+ hardware plus $30M+/day electricity. Economically irrational.
Spending the same bitcoin twice. Prevented by proof-of-work consensus. The core problem Bitcoin solves.
Block 0, mined by Satoshi on January 3, 2009. Contains the embedded message: 'Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks.'
'Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System' — 9 pages published October 31, 2008 by Satoshi Nakamoto.
Government-issued money not backed by a physical commodity. Value derived from legal decree. Every fiat currency in history has been debased.
School of economics emphasizing sound money, time preference, and free markets. Intellectual foundation of Bitcoin's design.
Money that is not susceptible to sudden changes in supply. Historically gold; now arguably Bitcoin.
Dollar-Cost Averaging. Buying a fixed dollar amount of Bitcoin on a regular schedule regardless of price. Reduces timing risk.
Satoshis. The smallest unit of Bitcoin: 1 sat = 0.00000001 BTC. Named after Satoshi Nakamoto.
Hold On for Dear Life. Originated from a drunken Bitcointalk typo in December 2013. Means: don't sell.
The 2015-2017 civil war over how to scale Bitcoin. SegWit (soft fork) won over big blocks (hard fork). Users defeated corporate interests.
2025 proposal to temporarily limit non-monetary data in Bitcoin transactions. Targets Ordinals/BRC-20. Most contentious debate since Blocksize War.
BIP 347. Proposal to re-enable data concatenation in Bitcoin script. Would enable covenants, vaults, and bridges.
A spending restriction that constrains how bitcoin can be spent in future transactions. Proposed via OP_CTV, OP_CAT, OP_VAULT.
